title: Linear optimal protocol for physical constraints in weakly driven processes
abstract: The minimization of irreversible work in weakly driven systems within linear response under physical constraints on the protocol derivative is studied. The problem reduces to a shifted eigenvalue equation involving the relaxation function. Owing to its dependence on time differences and its evenness, the relaxation kernel is naturally defined over a symmetric interval, where a periodic representation arises as a consistent closure that restores continuous translational invariance. Also, it shows how the irreversible work is defined in practice. Within this framework, the operator becomes diagonal in a Fourier basis. The global optimal solution is the zero mode, yielding a constant driving speed and a linear protocol. The corresponding optimal work depends only on the integrated relaxation function. Numerical results obtained via genetic programming confirm the robustness of this solution across different kernels.
summary: This work determines the minimum irreversible work required to drive a weakly coupled quantum system between states under specific constraints. By reformulating the problem using periodic boundary conditions, the authors show that the optimal driving protocol is universally linear. This result highlights that the minimum dissipation is dictated by the global memory properties of the system, independent of the specific details of the relaxation kernel.
main_problem: The paper minimizes the irreversible work done in weakly driven quantum systems while respecting physical constraints on the driving protocol's derivative.
main_result: The global optimal protocol is found to be linear, resulting in an optimal work value that depends only on the integrated relaxation function.
method: The problem is formulated as a constrained variational minimization, leading to a shifted eigenvalue equation solved by exploiting the periodicity of the relaxation kernel in a Fourier basis.
model_or_system: The study concerns weakly driven quantum systems analyzed within the framework of linear response theory. The dynamics are governed by an irreversible work functional involving a relaxation function $\Psi_0(t)$.
key_observables: Irreversible work ($	ext{Wirr}[v]$), the relaxation function $\Psi_0(t)$, and the optimal work ($	ext{W}^*_{	ext{irr}}$).
important_parameters: The driving time $	au$, the constraints on total variation ($\delta\lambda$), and the quadratic bound on the driving speed ($C$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system is in the weak driving regime and requires extending the relaxation kernel periodically to restore translational invariance.
figures_summary: Tables compare numerical results for the optimal work across various relaxation functions ($\exp(-|t|/	au_R)$, $J_0$, $\sin$, $\cos$), showing convergence to an analytical prediction. Figure 1 illustrates the resulting linear protocol.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the variational problem, derives the associated shifted eigenvalue equation, solves it using Fourier analysis on a periodic domain, and confirms the universal linear solution through numerical testing across multiple kernel types.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous, spectral understanding of optimal dissipation in non-equilibrium quantum processes, showing that simple linear protocols minimize work even when complex memory effects are present.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
